title: Fibre preparation with a hand hackle
subtitle: How to use a single-row hackle to align long-staple fibres and produce combed top for worsted spinning
excerpt: A hand hackle is a single-sided board set with long metal pins used to comb long-staple fibres into a parallel preparation called top. This guide covers how to load the hackle, comb through the fibres in passes, and draw the finished top off for spinning.
sourceNotes: Hackle combing is documented in Victorian textile preparation manuals and is a standard worsted preparation technique in UK and US spinning guilds. Observation-led; the draw-off technique is consistent across guild teaching.

glossaryTerms.definition: A tool for fibre preparation consisting of a board or stand fitted with upright metal pins. Fibre is loaded onto the pins and combed through to align parallel and remove short fibres and vegetable matter. A hand hackle has a single row of pins; a table hackle is fixed to a surface.
Fibre prepared by combing in which all fibres are aligned parallel, producing a smooth dense rope ready for worsted spinning. Top produces yarn with less halo and more sheen than carded roving.
A small disc with a central hole used to draw combed fibre off a hackle or drum carder into a consistent rope of top. The diz controls the diameter of the top as it is drawn.

content.text: is a comb with upright metal pins set in a row on a wooden or metal bar. Loading locks of long-staple fleece onto the pins and then combing through them in successive passes aligns all fibres parallel and removes short fibres (called noils), vegetable matter, and second cuts. The prepared fibre is then drawn off through a
ready for worsted spinning. The hackle suits fibres 10 cm and longer; shorter fibres are better prepared on hand cards.

attrs.title: Pin safety
attrs.body: Hackle pins are extremely sharp and long. Clamp the hackle firmly before use. Never press hands onto the pin field from above.

items.name: Hand hackle with at least one row of pins, clamped to a table edge
Long-staple locks, 10 cm or longer: Wensleydale, Romney, BFL, or similar
Diz (a small disc with a central hole) for drawing off top
Second hackle or hand combs for loading pass (optional)

content.text: Clamp the hackle firmly to the edge of the table. Take a lock of fleece and load the cut (skin) end onto the pins by pressing it gently down onto the pin tips, working toward the base. Do not push the fibre all the way to the base of the pins; leave the tips of the lock fibres free to comb. Load 6 to 8 locks side by side across the pin row without crowding the pins. Overcrowding compresses the fibres and makes subsequent passes difficult.

Take the second hackle or hand combs in the working hand. Begin at the tips of the loaded locks and pull through the fibre with light, sweeping strokes from tip toward the loaded base. Work the whole width of the hackle. After three or four light passes, the loose, tangled fibres will have transferred to the combing tool; the fibres remaining on the fixed hackle should be smooth and aligned. Progress in passes from tips inward: never force the comb into the base of the locks.

Load the fibre from the combing tool back onto the fixed hackle from the opposite end, and comb through again in the other direction. Repeat two or three transfer passes until the fibre sits smooth and parallel on the hackle with no tangles or crimp visible. Any short fibres and vegetable matter fall away at the base of the pins during combing.
Step 4: Draw off through the diz

To draw the combed top off the hackle, thread a few fibres from the tip end of the hackle through the central hole of the diz. Hold the diz between thumb and index finger close to the hackle face. With the other hand, pull the diz slowly away from the hackle while the diz hole controls the diameter of the rope forming behind it. Move the diz in a slow, even draw: pulling too fast produces thick-and-thin top; pulling too slowly makes the fibres compact. Wind the drawn top into a loose coil. The drawn top is ready for spinning.

items.problem: The fibre tears rather than combing smoothly
items.cause: The combing passes started too close to the base of the pins rather than at the tips
items.fix: Always begin at the outermost tips and work inward in successive passes. Starting at the base compresses rather than aligns the fibres.

items.problem: The drawn top is uneven with thick sections
items.cause: The diz was pulled unevenly or the draw speed was inconsistent
items.fix: Draw at a steady, even pace and keep the diz perpendicular to the hackle face throughout. The diz hole controls the diameter; the draw speed controls the density.
